items.rationale: He frames sum-check + multilinear polynomials (vs. univariate) as the true performance dividing line in SNARK design, unlocking a new design space where the prover only pays for non-zero terms.

citations.quote: The key performance dichotomy in SNARK design... It's the sum-check protocol and multilinear polynomials vs. univariate polynomials.
Sum-check unlocks a totally new design space compared to SNARKs based on univariate polynomials.

items.rationale: He believes Jolt's lookup-centric, sum-check-only design is a paradigm shift that matches or beats other zkVMs, and that what makes VMs good for hardware also makes them good for SNARKs.
items.age_note: Excitement is consistent across 2024-04 to 2025-01; tempered by his own 2025-03 caution on production readiness (see concerns).

citations.quote: Jolt isn't just a leap in speed; it's a paradigm shift.
Jolt continues to match or outperform other zkVMs, with folding set to preserve this advantage.

items.rationale: His newest primitives reduce memory-checking to massive but sparse constraint systems, promising ~3x end-to-end prover speedups and validating Jolt's core design bets — plus faster SNARKs outside zkVMs.

citations.quote: Expect ~3x end-to-end prover speedups (on top of 2x improvements that are already in progress), plus shorter proofs.
Twist & Shout validate our big design bets in Jolt and, as a bonus, give much faster SNARKs outside of the zkVM context too.

items.rationale: Maximal use of lookups is the source of both Jolt's simplicity and much of its performance; he turns every VM action into lookups/reads/writes.

citations.quote: it instantiates the lookup singularity, yielding the simplest and most extensible zkVM to date.
What sets Jolt apart? ...Simplicity comes from lookups; performance from both.

items.rationale: He argues binary or 256-bit fields beat 31/64-bit fields because committing to zeros is free/cheaper, and wants the community to adopt sum-check PIOPs with the Binius multilinear commitment.
items.age_note: Binius hope stated 2024-06-03; reinforced by the 2025-01-23 field-size argument.

citations.quote: 256-bit or binary fields are better than 31-bit or 64-bit fields for SNARK performance.
I hope the community adopts sum-check-based PIOPs combined with the Binius commitment for performant SNARKs.

items.rationale: Folding is the path past Jolt's biggest usability barrier (prover memory), enabling arbitrarily long RISC-V executions under 2GB — which he calls a 'phase change' — while preserving Jolt's speed edge.
items.age_note: Stated 2024-11-12 as in-progress; ~4.5 months old, not restated by 2025-03-31.

citations.quote: integrating Jolt with the Nova folding scheme, which will allow for arbitrarily long RISC-V executions with under 2 GB of prover memory

items.rationale: He prefers proving widely-scrutinized standard VMs/compilers over bespoke 'SNARK-friendly' designs, and is excited that techniques like D&P's bring much faster SNARKs for standard hashes like Keccak.

citations.quote: Twist & Shout bring the cost profile of SNARKs even closer to that of real CPUs.
this paves the way for much faster SNARKs for standard hash functions like Keccak.

items.rationale: Despite his own optimism, he warns the tech is not ready for complex, high-stakes deployments — bugs are everywhere and the field 'flirts with disaster' pretending otherwise.
items.age_note: Most recent and emphatic stance (2025-03-11/12); coexists with his structural optimism rather than reversing it.

citations.quote: while SNARKs and zkVMs show immense promise, they're not ready for complex, high-stakes deployments.
we flirt with disaster if we pretend they're ready for prime time.

items.rationale: Formal verification of proof systems is nascent; he says we can't call a zkVM 'secure' until verified verifiers exist (Stage 2), which is years off — even Zcash, far simpler than any zkVM, once had a major bug.

citations.quote: Until we reach Stage 2, we can't really call a zkVM 'secure'—and getting there is still likely several years off.
Zcash is about the limit of what I'd trust (absent formal verification), and it's still far simpler than any zkVM pipeline

items.rationale: Overhead versus native execution still exceeds 100,000×, a non-starter for most use cases; he proposes five 'performance stages' to cut this by orders of magnitude toward on-device proving.

citations.quote: overheads versus native execution still exceed 100,000×—a non-starter for most use cases.

items.rationale: He repeatedly argues purpose-built SNARK-friendly VMs only cater to today's SNARK limits and block reuse of mature, scrutinized tooling — a worse path than proving standard VMs compiled from languages like Rust.
items.age_note: Position held since 2024-05; restated 2025-01-23, so current.

citations.quote: Proposals for 'SNARK-friendly' VMs at best cater to today's SNARK limits, and prevent reuse of mature tooling.
the lack of a compiler does not mean that exposing that language to developers is a strict improvement over the Rust-->VM-bytecode approach.

items.rationale: He's concerned top-line benchmarks bundle proof system, engineering, hardware, and hand-tuned precompiles together, obscuring a system's fundamental efficiency and where the field actually stands.

citations.quote: many benchmarks bundle everything... into a single top-line number, obscuring where we really stand.
Benchmarking SNARKs involves complexities, but it's crucial to understand their innate performance characteristics.

items.rationale: Verifying evaluation proofs (e.g. Binius) on-chain can cost many millions of gas, forcing composition with a small-proof SNARK whose prover-side pain is still somewhat TBD.

citations.quote: people will probably want to compose with a small-proof SNARK rather than paying many millions of gas to verify evaluation proofs on-chain.

items.rationale: Not a market call — a security/trust comment. He cites Zcash as roughly the upper limit of SNARK deployment he'd trust absent formal verification, and notes it once had a major bug, signaling caution even for the most mature zk system.
items.age_note: His only token-adjacent reference; framed as a trust ceiling, not investment.

citations.quote: Zcash is about the limit of what I'd trust (absent formal verification)... Of course, even Zcash had a major bug in the past.

risk_regime.rationale: On near-term, high-stakes deployment of SNARK/zkVM technology his most recent posture (Mar 2025) is explicitly cautious: not production-ready, bugs everywhere, formal verification years away, 100,000×+ overhead. This is technology-maturity risk, not a market/token call (he expresses no view on prices or macro). Underneath the caution he is deeply, structurally optimistic about the long-run trajectory of sum-check-based SNARKs and Jolt.

notes: Justin Thaler is a16z crypto's cryptography researcher and the lead voice on the Jolt zkVM; his public statements are almost entirely about proof-system R&D, not tokens, sectors-as-investments, or macro risk. I mapped his views onto research/infrastructure 'sectors' accordingly. Strongest, most recent signal (Jan–Mar 2025): high conviction in sum-check + multilinear polynomials, Twist & Shout, and lookups; paired with a sharpened public caution that zkVMs are NOT yet safe for high-stakes use. No reversal detected — the March 2025 caution is a maturity warning layered on top of (not contradicting) his structural optimism. tokens_excited is empty because he names no tokens favorably; the single ZEC entry is a trust/security caveat rather than a market view. Much of the 2023–early-2024 corpus is technical debate (GKR/Plonk costs, prover mult counts) that informs but doesn't change the directional view. Folding/Nova excitement (Nov 2024) is the oldest still-active thread and was not restated by the 2025-03-31 cutoff, so flagged with age_note but treated as current since unreversed.
